pure information-theoretic explanation —treating text as a signal moving through a channel, not as “characters on a screen.”
These excerpts document a programmed method for producing an ‚Äúofficial‚Äù project reality from a network model, rather than a direct reconstruction of events. The system first defines completion as a probabilistic quantity (scheduled time mapped to a Z-score and cumulative probability), then enumerates a limited set of ‚Äúmajor‚Äù network paths with associated means and variances, and applies explicit stopping and selection criteria (e.g., discarding additional paths once their mean differs from the first path by more than a multiple of path standard deviations). It then computes pairwise correlations among the major paths and deletes highly correlated paths to retain a smaller ‚Äúrepresentative set,‚Äù thereby reducing the space of admissible explanations to those preserved by the correlation thresholding procedure. Simulation outputs and statistical summaries validate the model only within the model‚Äôs boundary conditions, after which scheduling software operationalizes the chosen representation through resource-loading histograms, cumulative usage plots, and dated line-printer activity schedules containing earliest/latest times and float. Finally, the organizational breakdown structure assigns responsibility to data production and updates, making the printed schedule/cost artifacts the enforceable reference state for coordination, reporting, and decision.
